title: Kosambari

overview.description: A fresh South Indian salad of soaked moong dal, cucumber, carrot, coconut, herbs and lemon finished with a fragrant mustard seed tempering.

ingredients: 2 medium carrots, grated or finely diced
50g fresh coconut, grated, or desiccated coconut rehydrated with a little water
½ tsp salt, or to taste
1 tbsp neutral oil or coconut oil
method: Cover with plenty of cold water.
Leave to soak for 1–2 hours until softened but still retaining some bite.
Drain the moong dal very thoroughly.
Place it in a large bowl.
Add the cucumber, carrot, coconut, coriander and green chilli if using.
Add the lemon juice and salt.
Heat the oil in a small frying pan over medium heat.
When they begin to pop, add the urad dal.
Cook until the urad dal is lightly golden.
Add the asafoetida and curry leaves.
Cook for a few seconds until fragrant.
Pour the tempering over the salad.
Taste and adjust the lemon juice and salt.

learning.did-you-know: Kosambari is particularly associated with Karnataka and is often served during festivals and celebrations. The moong dal is soaked rather than fully cooked, giving the salad its characteristic fresh texture.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How soaking legumes can create a fresh, crunchy salad ingredient and how a simple tempering can add depth and aroma to an otherwise raw dish.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A genuinely different salad that combines fresh vegetables with soaked moong dal and coconut, making it more substantial than many raw vegetable salads.
chapman-tips: Drain the moong dal extremely well; excess water will make the salad soggy.
The dal should be softened but retain a slight bite.
Don't leave the salad sitting in the dressing for too long before serving.
The tempering should be hot when poured over the salad so it releases the aroma of the spices.
Fresh coconut is excellent, but desiccated coconut works when necessary.
Adjust the lemon juice to taste; the salad should be bright and refreshing.
The green chilli is optional and can be left out for a milder family version.
The salad is particularly good with papad for contrasting crunch.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The soaked dal and vegetables lose their texture over time.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten the same day. Leftovers can be mixed into a wrap or served alongside rice.
